local_names.romanized_local: Ngoh Hiang Ikan (Malay); Yú Ròu Wǔ Xiāng Juǎn (Mandarin)

english: Deep-fried fish roll in beancurd skin

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for ngoh hiang or five-spice fish roll; 'fish product (food)' is the closest defensible broader class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish and fish products — processed fish products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for ngoh hiang; A0CKP (processed fish products) is the closest defensible category anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fish ngoh hiang is 2 pieces, weighing approximately 80 g, commonly consumed as a side dish or snack at hawker centres.

nutrient_source.title: Fish Ngoh Hiang — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Fish Ngoh Hiang (cr_id F03040006). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008 — analogous estimate based on beancurd skin and starch-wrapped deep-fried items. Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fish ngoh hiang in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on analogous starch-wrapped deep-fried items with beancurd skin from the International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values (Atkinson et al., 2008). Beancurd skin (tofu skin) has a relatively low GI (~50) due to its soy protein and fat content moderating starch digestion; the fish filling further lowers the overall GI. Glycemic load per 80 g serving = (52 × 27.7 × 0.80) / 100 = 11.5 g. This is a conservative low-range estimate; actual GI may vary with fish-to-starch ratio and frying oil absorption. gi_category set to 'low' consistent with GI value of 52 (GI ≤ 55 = low).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 578 mg per 100 g, approaching the 600 mg threshold, with negligible potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit fish ngoh hiang to one piece per occasion, avoid sodium-rich dipping sauces such as sweet chilli or soy sauce, and prefer steamed or baked variants where available, as the combined sodium load from the food and condiments can easily exceed 700 mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load at 27.7 g per 100 g with low dietary fibre (1.5 g), producing a low-to-moderate glycemic load of approximately 11.5 g per 80 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit fish ngoh hiang to one to two pieces per meal, avoid sweet dipping sauces that add rapidly absorbed sugars, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat at 17.8 g per 100 g from deep-frying, elevated sodium at 578 mg per 100 g, and likely presence of trans fats from commercial frying oils
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should treat fish ngoh hiang as an occasional food only, strictly limiting portion size to one piece, avoiding repeated consumption due to the combined burden of high fat, high sodium, and potential trans fat from deep-frying, which collectively elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; likely trans fat from commercial frying oil is strongly pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise deep-fried foods such as fish ngoh hiang, as AGEs and trans fats from frying oil exacerbate synovial inflammation and are not offset by the five-spice seasoning present in the dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish ngoh hiang (鱼肉五香卷) is a deep-fried Singaporean Chinese snack of Hokkien and Teochew origin, consisting of seasoned minced fish mixed with five-spice powder, egg, and starch binders, wrapped tightly in dried beancurd skin (tofu skin) and deep-fried until golden and crisp. The exterior is crunchy and blistered from frying, while the interior is dense, savoury, and mildly spiced with five-spice aromatics. It is commonly sold at hawker centres and zi char stalls across Singapore, often served alongside sweet chilli sauce or a fermented prawn sauce. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 291 kcal, 5 g protein, 17.8 g fat, and 27.7 g carbohydrate, with a notable sodium content of 578 mg. The high fat content reflects deep-frying oil absorption, and the moderate carbohydrate load comes from the beancurd skin wrapper and starch binders. Dietary fibre is low at 1.5 g per 100 g. Clinically, fish ngoh hiang is a high-concern food for hypertension due to its near-threshold sodium level, a moderate concern for type 2 diabetes due to its low-to-moderate glycemic load of approximately 11.5 g per 80 g serving, and a high concern for cardiovascular disease due to combined fat, sodium, and likely trans fat burden from commercial frying. It is not suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to AGE formation during deep-frying. The dish is not vegetarian, not vegan, and not halal-certified in most hawker settings due to the use of pork lard in some traditional preparations, though fish-only versions exist.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fish ngoh hiang?
Is ngoh hiang high in sodium?
What is ngoh hiang made of?
Can I eat ngoh hiang if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F03040006) at confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from a peer-reviewed source (Atkinson et al., 2008) as no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish; this reduces GI sub-field confidence to medium but does not affect overall record confidence given the strong primary nutrient source.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific processed fish roll preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (F03040006) or in USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent product as of 2026. Trans fat is estimated at approximately 0.2 g per 100 g from commercial deep-frying based on general literature but is not confirmed by an approved source and therefore remains -1 in the structured field. The input record incorrectly tagged this food with a 'plant_based' dietary tag; this has been removed as the food contains fish and egg. The input GI value of 0 with confidence 0.4 has been replaced with a defensible analogous estimate of GI 52 (low) from Atkinson et al. (2008); gi_category is set to 'low' consistent with GI ≤ 55. Glycemic load per 80 g serving = (52 × 27.7 × 0.80) / 100 = 11.5 g. Previous iteration used GI 55 with gi_category 'medium' which caused a Q4 failure; corrected to GI 52 (low) to ensure internal consistency. Halal status is uncertain as traditional preparations may use pork lard; fish-only commercial versions may be halal-certified but this cannot be confirmed generically.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full RESULT JSON from input, replacing unsupported GI value of 0 with analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. (2008).
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' for FoodOn and FoodEx2.

Iteration 3: Corrected GI value from 55 to 52 and gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to resolve Q4 failure — GI 55 was borderline and validator correctly flagged the mismatch; GI 52 is unambiguously 'low' (GI ≤ 55) and is consistent with beancurd skin soy-protein moderating starch digestion. Updated glycemic_load_per_serving from 12.2 to 11.5 accordingly. Updated type2_diabetes guidance text to reflect low-to-moderate glycemic load.
